questions.explanation: IDEs commonly provide syntax highlighting and real-time linting/diagnostics to flag syntax issues immediately.
questions.question: In an IDE, what feature most directly helps you catch syntax errors as you type?

questions.explanation: git checkout -b <name> both creates the branch and checks it out.
questions.question: Which Git command creates a new branch named "feature/login" and switches to it in one step?

questions.explanation: let is block-scoped and allows reassignment; const is block-scoped but not reassignable.
questions.question: In JavaScript, which keyword declares a block-scoped variable that can be reassigned?

questions.explanation: GET is intended for safe, read-only retrieval of resources.
questions.question: Which HTTP method is typically used to retrieve data without changing server state?

questions.explanation: A RESTful design uses resource-oriented paths (e.g., /products) and GET for retrieval; pagination fits naturally as query parameters.
questions.question: You are designing a REST API for a UI that lists products and supports pagination. Which endpoint design is most appropriate?

questions.explanation: 1NF requires atomic values; a separate related table removes repeating groups and stores one phone number per row.

options.A: Add an index on the phone number column
options.B: Move phone numbers into a separate table with one row per phone number and a foreign key to the user
options.C: Store phone numbers as a comma-separated string

questions.question: In database normalization, which change best resolves a repeating group (e.g., multiple phone numbers stored in one column) to reach 1NF?

questions.explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps all customers and counts matching orders; customers with no orders produce NULLs that COUNT(o.id) treats as 0.

options.A: SELECT c.id, COUNT(o.id) FROM customers c INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.id;
options.B: SELECT c.id, COUNT(o.id) FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.id;
options.C: SELECT c.id, SUM(o.id) FROM customers c RIGHT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.id;
options.D: SELECT c.id, COUNT(*) FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id;

questions.question: In SQL, which query returns the number of orders per customer, including customers with zero orders?

questions.explanation: HttpOnly cookies are not readable by JavaScript, reducing XSS token exfiltration risk; short-lived tokens and server-managed sessions/refresh flows further limit exposure.

options.A: Store the access token in localStorage and reuse it until it expires
options.B: Store the access token in sessionStorage and reuse it until it expires
options.C: Use an HttpOnly, Secure cookie for a short-lived session (or refresh token) and keep access tokens out of JavaScript-accessible storage
options.D: Embed the access token into the HTML at build time

questions.question: A single-page app (SPA) uses OAuth2 with JWT access tokens. Which approach best reduces the risk of token theft via XSS while still allowing the UI to call APIs?

questions.explanation: A broker/stream decouples producers/consumers, buffers bursts, and enables reliable event-driven updates suitable for real-time UI read models.

options.A: Have the UI poll the database every second
options.B: Use a message broker/stream (e.g., RabbitMQ/Kafka) to publish notification events and have a consumer update a read model for the UI
options.C: Write notifications to a shared file on disk and have the UI read it
options.D: Send events by embedding them in image responses

questions.question: You have a UI that shows a real-time notification badge. The backend emits events when notifications change. Which architecture is most appropriate to decouple services and handle bursts reliably?

questions.explanation: Docker Compose provides reproducible multi-service environments with consistent service discovery and environment configuration, ideal for CI integration testing.

options.A: Hardcode the API URL in the UI code and commit it
options.B: Run everything on the host machine and manually start services in the right order
options.C: Use Docker Compose to define UI/API/DB services, networks, and environment variables, and run tests against the composed stack
options.D: Disable integration tests in CI and rely only on unit tests

questions.question: Your UI integration tests pass locally but fail in CI because the API base URL differs per environment. You want a repeatable setup that runs the UI, API, and database together with consistent networking. What is the best solution?
